speaker: interviewer
text: --Ludwig Weiler. We're in London. This is tape six.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. I just would like to go back in the period of-- in-- of 1940, '41 period when, I think I mentioned earlier-- and please correct me if I said the story-- when we had to prove our Hungarian nationality. You see, there were Jews who escaped from Austria-- unfortunately for them, in the wrong direction-- to Hungary from Czechoslovakia, and also in 1940 from Poland. And many of them tried to get Aryan papers. Others they k-- like Polish soldiers because there was a friendship treaty, so to speak, between Hungary and Poland. So they were in some difficulty, the Hungarians, at that point, when the Germans marched into Poland. But nevertheless, a lot of Jews also came over. Now, obviously, the police in the Hungarian government-- because you had the-- a reporting duty that anybody who lived in any house had to be reported within 24 hours that he's living there-- got to know that a lot of Jews have come in, particularly from Poland and also from these other two countries. And therefore, the Hungarian government made a special effort to pursue the policy of proving your nationality. We did. We had no problem. We'd proven it, in fact, back up to 1820 when the records started. So I'm not talking of personal problems. But what happened was that anybody else who they caught who could not prove it. And they have records, they knew where people lived. Because up to that point, people were not worried about that they're going to do anything. They caught them. They interned them in Hungary. And in due course, they deported them to Kamianets-Podilskyi, is the place called in Poland where they were, in 1942, shot. We found it out at the later stage.

speaker: interviewee
text: So therefore, the Hungarians have excelled themselves already to, to deport people before the actually the Germans came in to occupy the country. So there is no question about the guilt and complicity in the deportation of the Hungarian Jewry.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was another point I'm just forgetting. I only made a few words of notes, which I wanted to add there. The-- we were geographically in Schoppernau, literally a pan handle into Austria. And in 1941 when the Germans-- in April '41-- invaded Yugoslavia, I have personally watched how the German units on trucks, motorcycles, tanks were going through the main thoroughfare of the town towards down to Yugoslavia. Today, that's a question of an hour and a half to maximum two hour journey. Maybe those days, it took a bit longer. And literally in no time, they overran us, you know, Yugoslavia and Greece. And in that year, in 1941 already and in '42, similarly, trains were coming with Yugoslavian Jews through our town, and also Greek Jews. I remember distinctly that I stood at the railway-- next to the railway station where you could see a clear picture of these wagons which are run on-- not wagon [INAUDIBLE] wagons, but how they were transported. We didn't know where to. The story was that they're going to go to work camps. And, and there is a place called-- just a moment-- [NON-ENGLISH]. I forget the name of it. And there were cards received in Hungary by people from family or whatnot who were, in fact, deported to some of the death camps. Where the Germans made them write camp that we are fine, we arrived, and this and that and the other.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was a great deal of deception going on. I remember very clearly when we were told by one of the railway employees that this train came from, from Athens carrying Jews from Crete. Can you imagine? So I literally saw those trains going. Lit-- You saw the trains. Presumably, you couldn't see any people because there were no windows.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear anything from them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Because look, in one-- imagine yourself there is a alternate to a railway station. And it was in the-- the railway station was not very far where we lived. And I didn't go out to the platform, because obviously, they didn't allow anybody. They knew that there was a train of some importance going through. But next to the railway station there was a clearing where, where the railway lines were running. And I was within maybe 40, 50 meters from where the railway lines were running. But obviously, these tracks and this tiny little hole, and God knows how long those people have been already on the train. They were not leaning out on it. And all I'm trying to say is that we knew when-- which later became known as deportations-- these transports were going through our town. I mean, even I, I remember very clearly when the African campaign of Roma was going on. Similarly, the sand painted tanks and armored vehicles on train tracks were going through our town. You see, it was a junction from Germany, Austria down to the south. Anyway, so we were none the wiser what happened to them. We certainly didn't know what. And I told you about the SS brigades who went from our town as volunteers and so on. There was no-- look, by the time it came to '41, '42 Hungary was land-locked by all German occupied country-- Austria to the west, Slovakia to the north, Czechoslavakia, Yugoslavia to the south. There was a common border with Romania. The Romanians, strangely enough, did not deport Jews. Even so, certain atrocities, like in Iasi, have happened. But, of course, Romanians were always known that they are on the take. And for some reason or other, the Romanian king didn't allow mass deportations which happened all around the countries.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this is basically my story. I, I could give you some minor anecdotes that, for instance, the Hungarian army commandeered all the bicycles for the army. So they took our bicycles away. Six months, nine months later, they gave us back a broken piece of bicycle. You can imagine what sort of war they were fighting, the Hungarians, like bows and arrows, you know, on bicycles.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can give you another example. They, they took all the horses in, in, in the outside villages and brought it in to a place and they took them to the army. Just opposite was a big sort of a clearing, a park-- not a park, really, but trees and grass opposite the shul, the orthodox shul. And I remember it was this time of the year when there is late [NON-ENGLISH] and the [NON-ENGLISH]. There's a time in between. They wait for the [NON-ENGLISH] And I fell. We were chasing each other. I fell down, and I hurt myself, so I had to have a tetanus injection. So I got it, and I got all blown up. And seemingly, it didn't agree with me. You know, there are many childhood anecdotes which really have not a great deal to do with the war. All I would like to say is that we were very much aware what was happening. And we read the newspapers. We used to listen to the BBC. My father understood a bit of English, and even others did. That was highly dangerous because that was a criminal offense. Once the Germans came in, it was basically-- Jews were, I don't think, allowed to use radios. They came in on the 19th of March. We were at home still on Pesach. And I think I looked up the dates with you in a 100 year calendar recently. We were taken out of the ghetto into the half-built brick factory on the 29th of June. We must have been in the ghetto approximately five, or six, or maybe seven weeks maximum, which was in the-- there were two ghettos in town. One was near where the orthodox shul was, which was a high wooden fence, and people were stopped to get in or out. And the other one was in the center of town, which is a very old part of it and called [NON-ENGLISH]. So one street just with two houses. That is the one which we were in. That is where I mentioned that in the Communist time they discovered the medieval old synagogue, which dates back from year 1100 or before. And we were taken out of a very comfortable home into given a room with my parents and my brother and myself with meager belongings and doing nothing. But we were taken out-- anybody who was of working age, or who could volunteer-- to do agricultural work in the nearby fields. Now, this is-- this brings me to an interesting point. And I also went out. First of all, you went out in the fresh air. You were not cooped up in there. And so my father, my brother and I went. The next older brother of my father [? Uli ?] is Gyula, who was the father of Gershon, the professor, my cousin, who survived in the Munka Tabor. Was in the First World War as a soldier. And I remember we used to make-- poke fun at him that he used to tell always his military stories. Nevertheless, for some reason or other, he was the only one-- and we didn't take him sometimes seriously with all his war stories-- he exactly said about Auschwitz and what's happening there. And nobody believed him, including my parents-- nobody. They considered him half crazy. So much so that on the 29th of June, when the ghetto was cleared and taken out to near the railway station into a half-built factory, he left his wife and daughter, and he escaped into the forest. He somehow or other maintained himself for two, three months. Eventually one of the Hungarian forest guides gave him-- reported him. He was taken to some sort of a internment camp. And from there, even at the later stage in August when he was sent to Auschwitz, and he never came back. You know, the, the, the, the-- I don't call it religiousness, but the, the, the enthusiasm with which the Hungarians pursued the German final solution is equal to none. They did a perfect job. And if you really realize what administration it must've taken to take 650,000 people out from villages of towns and organize railways and, and, and register them, and take their properties away, and everything in a matter of three months-- this is a feat not equaled in many history of organization. And it was only possible with the total cooperation and willingness of the Hungarians. So that is all I've got left for Hungary, and the rest is really not material. Now, I go on to come into the period of 1941, '42. There were a lot of people who came back injured-- Jewish people who were in the Munka Tabor, the work camps in the Ukraine. And that, I must say, shocked me. You know, somebody coming back on crutches, and he was a healthy, strong man six months earlier before he was taken out. But there were lucky that they were still let back for recuperations.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it surprising that your brother being six years older than you, how did he--

speaker: interviewee
text: I will come-- I will tell you exactly what happened. And that I remember a man called Neufeld. There was a number of brothers Neufeld. They had-- one had a fur business. One was a jeweler. And those brothers actually survived afterwards. And they went out to South America to Sao Paolo, and they became extremely wealthy in the jewelry business. And I met a relation of theirs only a year ago. And that was the first man I remember. He came back like a war cripple. Maybe his injuries were not as bad as given the impression of my age. And that brought home really that the war was going on, and Jews were sufficiently suffering. So that was the first thing. You say about my brother was six years older. This is absolutely true. He matriculated in 1942 at the age of 18. They were going to take him into the work camps. There were a lot of people-- or everybody-- tried some method of trying to get out of it. Many people made themselves ill. They took medication and contracted lung infections. Others lost even their sight. It's unbelievable what people did because it was considered the semi-death sentence if you were taken into the Munka Tabor and in turn sent to Ukraine.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there were two stages. Once you were in Munka Tabor, you didn't know where they were sending you. Some stayed in Hungary. Not all of them were sent out. It went through [INAUDIBLE], or whichever method they used because they used them for building railways and various jobs in the country as well, which was not as lethal as the Ukraine or the Russian front, but certainly no pleasure.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my brother, having just completed his matric and his picture being in a most elegant shop window with all his friends being a perfectly normal person, has, has tried to, to, to, to learn to imitate a mental illness, i.e. Depression. Because depression is very difficult to, to detect, as you know, medicine-- people can tell you. And this is an up and down feeling. And I remember that he had to go to Gyor-- which was quite a distance-- to a mental institute that he was a inmate of there. And it was also a dangerous exercise because the military doctors who were not-- were the worst anti-Semites, who practically made half-dead people being fit to be taken in. They, they-- as the years went on, in '42, '43, it became politically impossible to get out of. Used the method that where they suspected that somebody was faking a mental illness, they sent them up to Budapest to one of the institutes, and they gave them electric shock treatments. Now, that's not funny when you are well. It's not funny when you're not well as well, but at least it does have a certain sort of improving effect, but he didn't. What we we considered was lucky that he got out of the Munka Tabor, and he never went into it, became a disaster afterwards because he got deported. So it was really a vicious circle. Now, the, the, the period of the mid-40s, you must understand that, for instance, my father in a couple of properties which we had, which were permanent properties in the city, had a cinema advertising board, which entitled us-- they gave us packs of tickets-- to any performance. And even if it was a full house, they put in two chairs. Anytime, we could go. Till this day, I'm not a keen cinema goer. I go what interests me. But I was never a child who needed to do some tricks to get to the cinema because all I had to do was to go to the wardrobe or, or in the drawer and take out as many tickets as I wanted. We had-- But the reason I'm telling you that is there were two cinemas in town and a very large theatre, which my mother used to take me. That the propaganda films of the Germans-- which was played in German because in our town they didn't even have to translate it. You know, didn't have to have subtitles on it-- were absolutely unbelievably effective. You know, some of the films-- I forget the name of it-- where Jews are compared to rats, you know, in their disgusting manner. Now, this brutalizing of the Jews was systematically fed all through the war. And they didn't need really a lot of prompting, I must say. So this is another little anecdote which I can add to it. But generally speaking, all I can say is that on that 19th of March, which was a Sunday in 1944, I got up from a comfortable bed. I look out the window. And because we were literally a kilometer from the border, at about 8:30 or 8:00, I see German military walking past. They were not in tanks or trucks or anything. They were just walking around. They were walking in, in, in lines of four, just one line of four people next to each other, which took up half of the road-- not the pavement, on the road. And that is the way how they sort of announced their presence. This was the, the visual impression which we got. And I realized that everybody got terribly worried and upset and telephoning left right and center, this is happening, that's happening. And then within days the machinery started. As I told you, my uncle got arrested, and the Judenrat got appointed. Everybody had to report any details. Lists and lists were made. The Germans dictated to the Judenrat, which they dutifully carried out by quickly registering every person who attended the feat. And so they knew where the Jews were, not that it could not have been eventually found out. But strangely enough, the Jews contributed-- OK, maybe under certain threat-- to be good boys and give everything to them. And if we give them that, everything will be all right. This is not what happened. Anyway, coming to the point of the deportation because I have told you that we were a few days in this brick building, which had no windows. Food was not even available very much, just very little.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything about the ghetto to say? You were there for a number of weeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the ghetto, strange enough, we were-- we were lacking somewhat the food. I do not remember that we were actually going absolutely starving hungry. We had limited supplies of things. I cannot tell you in all honesty how the supplies came into the ghetto, or what was allocated. Presumably certain rations were given against payment, which they demanded for every-- they practically demanded to pay your fare into Auschwitz, not that they did. And our existence was pretty miserable there already. That was no fun anymore. And, and everybody went around with a drawn face and hoping what's going to happen. And we, we, we still knew about the Allied landings on the 6th of June in 1944. We were still there in the ghetto. And we were hoping for the best, that it's literally coming to an end, and nothing serious may happen. And the story was that we will be taken to work in the east. The fear in the people was that we are taken to Poland. Let me just tell you the actual deportation train, and then I want to come back to this question of Poland otherwise. It was on a summer afternoon when we were told that we have to go in groups, walk to the station, which was maybe 10 minutes walk from where we were. It was very close to our home, just up towards the [NON-ENGLISH]. That's the resort part of the town. And we went to the daily [INAUDIBLE], which means the southern station. And we were lined up and then told to get in, and 80, 90 people. I remember the people who were in our train. And I got into a corner. Being a young boy, I was just under the opening, which was quite helpful because as the train went, being a boy, I pulled myself up, and I could look out. And I used to tell the people, because everybody was very squashed there. So a grownup could hardly have made himself room to move his arms and his legs to, to pull himself up. And I as a child could in a second pull myself up and look out and could hold myself inside, or somebody help me to hold me. Now, when these deportations happened, my uncle and the other few people who were arrested by the gestapo as soon as they entered the town, were brought directly to the train, and they were put on the same train. The story is that the Judenrat from Budapest organized a so-called Palestina Transport. People who were of Zionist connection and background were supposed to have been seven to 10 families per large ghetto taken out off the deportation trains, of the various provincial centers where the deportations went on, and taken to Budapest into a ghetto where they were not to be deported. My uncle, Dr. Bela Weiler, had a very close Zionist association. I think he was on the first Hungarian Zionist Conference. And he was near enough in contemporary, or just a little bit younger than Herzl. And Herzl, as you know, was Vienna-Budapest origin. And when he was taken to the train-- it's very unfortunate, but when it comes to important things, people, I can't say really anything terrible. But its, it's very hard on the people who it happened to that lists of the people which were sent out from Budapest have been changed by the people who got hold of it. And I do know of a family in another town-- name not to be mentioned because the children are still around-- who were practically chased out of the town after the war because their family put others on the train and put themselves in. Anyway, even our rabbi who was a very anti-Zionist man, got in on it, and literally he could not return to our town after the war. He stayed in Budapest. Anyway, these are unfortunate circumstances. And when people are put to test, a lot of things happen which probably in normal circumstances they would have never done. Anyway, now the train went for two and a half days. We got joined part of the Szombathely ghetto onto the train. And as we went towards northeast-- it's still in western Hungary-- the Gyor ghetto was also consolidated on the same train. So as it went through-- part of the Szombathely ghetto was transferred to the Sopron ghetto, joined up, because there were not enough people. And once they made up a train, they wanted to make it worthwhile.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Gyor ghetto-- so basically, the Western Hungarian corner towards Austria was one deportation train which left on the 4th of July 1944. The journey-- 4th? I think so, approximately that. And the train arrived in Birkenau on-- at approximately 9:00 in the morning on the Shabbat morning, which was the Jewish date of [NON-ENGLISH] the fast day.

speaker: interviewee
text: But where I want to tell you that people were, were worried if the word Poland was mentioned, that they're getting sent there, is that I remember clearly. We were at 3:00 in the morning in Krakow. I saw the station writing. You know, I was pushed up. I looked out. Approximately 8-something-- I don't even know how I know. Yes, I know the, the times because all the stations had clocks. We didn't have watches at the time. And I remember the name of the station being written out in German Gothic letters, Auschwitz. We were delighted because we thought we were in Germany. Little did we know what Auschwitz today means. I'm just telling You how blind the people were purely by being misled or disinformed, not being told the truth. And this has been a great argument amongst many learned and informed people, if you like to call it an accusation against the leaders of at the time. I will come in to tell you-- because I think we are coming to the end of this tape-- how the arrival actually took place.
